Dardenay considers the question of the extent to which the Aeneid might have influenced the iconographic representation of Rome's founding heroes. Gagliardi (2023a) notes that in his duels after the death of Pallas, Aeneas often addresses his dying or dead adversaries with cruel words, in contrast to their appeals to his pietas. Sometimes the dying enemies respond to his sarcasm in a noble way that underscores their emotional superiority. Gagliardi (2023b) notes that the verb immolare appears only three times in Vergil's entire work, all in the Aeneid and always referring to the human sacrifices Aeneas makes in his anger at the death of Pallas in 10.519 and 10.541 and in the murder of Turnus at the end of the poem (12.949). Morgan brings out the further implications of these passages: when Aeneas sacrifices the sons of Ufens at Aen. 10.515-20, Vergil deploys a name expressive of Roman citizenship itself. Ufens evokes the tribus Oufentina, established in 318 BCE as one of the thirty-five voting tribes Vergilian Bibliography 2024-2025 - 105 of Rome. Not only is Aeneas indulging in barbaric ritual practice, but in the process the founder of Rome is apparently attacking the very fabric of Rome. The implication in immolat (Aen. 12.949), Aeneas's word when he says that his slaying of Turnus is a sacrifice, introduces a comparable disquiet. Gallego Cebollada's book studies the various stylistic ways in which Vergil, Horace, and Ovid create portraits of their characters. Giusti argues that Dido in the Aeneid is both the victim of the Romans' racializing of Phoenicians and Carthaginians, and herself an actor of the Phoenicians' racializing of autochthonous people in North Africa. Skoutelas and Smith observe that in Ecl. 6 and G. 4-two poems in which youths bind an old figure with chains to compel a song-Vergil mobilizes the clash of youth and senectitude to comment on the effects of literary tradition on his own work. Van Geel discovers significance in Vergil's seemingly rushed treatment of two minor characters when they are considered in light of Vergil's use of Homeric epic in the intertext-heavy books 5 and 9. These characters function as Vergil's illustrations of Aeneas's movement away from the Homeric world, as the text progresses toward the proto-Roman project that features prominently in the Aeneid's narrative. An analysis of historical and archaeological data from the sixth to the second century BCE relating to the legend of Aeneas and his landing on coastal Latium leads D'Alessio and De Stefano to point to two defining periods: an older, mythic period, and a period coinciding with the Roman expansionist policy that preceded the renewed interest in Aeneas in the Vergilian age. Giusti takes up again the supposedly outdated debate about the pro- or antiVergilian Bibliography 2024-2025 - 107 Augustan stance of Augustan texts, which Duncan Kennedy's reflections on these points managed to cast in a different light: "'Augustan' and 'AntiAugustan': Reflections on Terms of Reference," in A. Powell, ed., Roman Poetry and Propaganda in the Age of Augustus (1992). Since then, the dichotomy seems useless in a dialectic that must always consider Augustus as a point of reference. Yet scholarship remains divided in its interpretation of Augustus's reign and its effects on poetry. Kennedy's study offers further reflections on the workings of Augustan ideology than previously assumed. A combined rereading of that essay and Hannah Arendt's writings on the ideological language of totalitarian systems (The Origins of Totalinarianism, 1951) opens up new possibilities for interpreting Augustan rule and anti-Augustanism in Augustan texts. Gregori observes that the prophecies, names of peoples, and military actions evoked in the Aeneid are not entirely conventional but are intended to recall historical events chronologically close and referable to Augustan Rome. This argument is confirmable through the evidence of Latin inscriptions and, in particular, through Augustus's Res gestae 25-33. Livorsi picks out a complex web of intertextual references ultimately dependent on Vergil, beginning with the observation that late antique laws are often rhetorically bolstered by expressions of popularized philosophy and theology. In a constitution of Valentinian III against tomb violators (Nouell. Valent. 23), the phrase nec uana fides, which features in the preamble of this law, seems merely an ornamental allusion to Aen. 4.12. The passage in the law might, however, be a reminiscence of Prudentius, Cath. 3.196, who had used the same Vergilian tag to allude to Paul (1 Cor 15:13-17), thus expressing faith in the doctrine of resurrection. The author of the Vergilian cento Versus ad gratiam Domini also redeployed the phrase nec uana fides with reference to the immortality of the soul, and the cento's intertextual relationship with Prudentius may contribute to the evidence about its date. This net of intertextual references (Vergil, Paul, Prudentius) bestows authority on the legal admonition and justifies an appreciation of the constitutions as literature. Maticic finds a connection between Hercules's drainage of landscapes and drying up of bodies and the evocation of hydraulic works and water consumption in Rome in the Augustan period. Vergilian Bibliography 2024-2025 - 111 Berno's analysis of both the content and the form of Seneca's quotations from Vergil demonstrates that Seneca uses them not only to convey Stoic ideas but also to provide a narrative. He prefers passages focused on a few key concepts: virtue and fighting, god and fate, death. These are main themes both of the Aeneid and of the epistles. The distribution of the themes throughout the collection aims in the beginning at improving one's virtue and then proceeds, toward the end, to an acceptance of death. Boureau observes that in Epist. 127 Augustine adopts the phrase uoti reus from Aen. 5.237 to consolidate the Christian concept of the vow (for the vow of chastity in marriage) by grounding it in the idea of a good debt and rediscovering the republican idea of civic engagement. Celotto notes that Persius engages with G. 1.145-49 via a "divided allusion," evoking this passage at two different moments in his collection (5.37-40 and prol. 10- 11) to set up a contrast between body and mind as a bad and good teacher of morals and literature, respectively. Erskine points out that Nemesianus's Eclogues are the last collection of bucolic poems before the sociopolitical shifts of the fourth century CE. Within his reuse of characters, tropes, and narrative structures from Vergil and Calpurnius Siculus, however, resides a consciousness of contemporary issues. The thematic structure of his first Eclogue can be analyzed with a view to his historical context: the eclogue finds recourse not in hoping for a new political golden age but in the consolatory and preservative power of a poetry oriented toward the past. Hardie points to the fact that Prudentius's Psychomachia is an allegorical epic as the feature that marks a decisive break with the tradition of classical epic. Yet this raises the question of the extent to which its allegorical techniques are, in fact, Vergilian. The question then arises as to whether reading Psychomachia with the help of the Aeneid can also provide insights for reading the Aeneid. Against this background, Vergil's use of allegorical techniques can be identified, which can be provocatively described as Prudentian. Hudson considers the literary aims of Vergilius orator an poeta?, the fragmentary dialogue attributed to Florus. Spence argues that at Conf. 5.8.15 Augustine uses our recognition of the affiliation between Conf. 5 and Aen. 4 to start us thinking about Dido, but then he moves us to another section of the Aeneid and rebuilds the landscape of Carthage into the landscape of the underworld. Tanozzi explores the theme of Priam's death: in Aen. 2, in Quintus of Smyrna's Posthomerica, and in Triphiodorus's Ilii excidium, Priam's death at the hands of Neoptolemus follows different narrative patterns, but all the descriptions adopt, and reverse, the model of the duel, so that the Trojan king's death departs from the heroic model. Tomcik (2024a) argues for the influence of Vergil's Fama (Aen. 4.173-97) on the opening scene of Valerius Flaccus's sixth book (6.1- 112 - Shirley Werner 30) and offers a metapoetic interpretation of the passage by reading Fama not only as a personification of Rumor but also as a representation of the literary tradition. Tomcik (2024b) finds that Fama is also a model for the appearance of Virtus at the outset of Menoeceus's sacrifice in Statius, Th. 10.610-80. In the Argonautica, Tzounakas demonstrates, Valerius treats some episodes in a way that could be interpreted as a commentary on relevant passages of the Aeneid. Particular focus is paid to passages that are presented as prequels to the Aeneid. This strategy allows Valerius to safeguard his originality and at the same time it reinforces his suggestion (with metaliterary implications) that the Argo is the first ship. Sowers examines late antique Greek and Latin centos from the perspective of the poets who wrote them and the authors who read Vergilian Bibliography 2024-2025 - 113 them, with attention given to Ausonius's preface and epilogue to his Cento nuptialis, Aelia Eudocia's prefatory poem to her Homeric cento, and the proem of Proba's Vergilian cento. It has been recognized, as Watson (2024a) explains, that the compositional strategy used by Proba reflects the exegetical method of typology: by reusing particular Vergilian verses for biblical characters, Proba creates a typological relationship whereby a Vergilian type both prefigures and is fulfilled by a biblical antitype. Her article now proposes an extended model of typology, in which the type not only prefigures the antitype, but also enfigures it, providing the reader with a conceptual paradigm through which to understand a supernatural reality. In the baptism scene (380-414), which serves as a case study, Proba reuses passages that in the Aeneid describe female characters, hinting at a feminine typological Trinity. Watson (2024b) writes that Proba's Cento contains a corruption at line 42, sometimes printed as two half-lines separated by a lacuna. Cowan suggests that Vergil's engagement at Aen. 2.309-17 with Callimachus's depiction of the sack of Callion by the Gauls in the Hymn to Delos (Del. 178-185) complicates his own depiction of the sack of Troy. Less securely, Vergil's inclusion of the figure of Ucalegon from Il. 3 may also evoke the οὐκ ἀλέγοντες Phlegyans from the Homeric Hymn to Apollo (Ap. 279), who disturbingly resemble both the Callian and Trojan victims and the Gallic and Greek aggressors. Fratantuono (2023) speculates that the murderous shapeshifting folkloric monsters known from quotations in Aristophanic comedy might have inspired Vergil in his portrayal of Dido's metamorphosis from a responsible ruler of her people to a furious enemy. Mercury's misogynistic warning to Aeneas about the mutability of women may also allude to the changing nature of these monsters. The polymath Xenophon is an undervalued source for certain passages in the Aeneid, as Fratantuono (2024a) notes. He is a literary precursor to Vergil's decisions both to emphasize Aeneas's hunting skills and to give bees a prominent place in his depiction of the emerging Carthage. Berno's analysis of both the content and the form of Seneca's quotations
from Vergil demonstrates that Seneca uses them not only to convey Stoic
ideas but also to provide a narrative. He prefers passages focused on
a few key concepts: virtue and fighting, god and fate, death. These are
main themes both of the Aeneid and of the epistles. The distribution of
the themes throughout the collection aims in the beginning at improving
one's virtue and then proceeds, toward the end, to an acceptance of death.
Boureau observes that in Epist. 127 Augustine adopts the phrase uoti reus
from Aen. 5.237 to consolidate the Christian concept of the vow (for the
vow of chastity in marriage) by grounding it in the idea of a good debt and
rediscovering the republican idea of civic engagement. Celotto notes that
Persius engages with G. 1.145-49 via a "divided allusion," evoking this
passage at two different moments in his collection (5.37-40 and prol. 10-

of morals and literature, respectively. Erskine points out that Nemesianus's
Eclogues are the last collection of bucolic poems before the sociopolitical
shifts of the fourth century CE. Within his reuse of characters, tropes,
and narrative structures from Vergil and Calpurnius Siculus, however,
resides a consciousness of contemporary issues. The thematic structure of
his first Eclogue can be analyzed with a view to his historical context: the
eclogue finds recourse not in hoping for a new political golden age but
in the consolatory and preservative power of a poetry oriented toward
the past. Hardie points to the fact that Prudentius's Psychomachia is an
allegorical epic as the feature that marks a decisive break with the tradition
of classical epic. Yet this raises the question of the extent to which its
allegorical techniques are, in fact, Vergilian. The question then arises as
to whether reading Psychomachia with the help of the Aeneid can also

Dardenay considers the question of the extent to which the Aeneid might have influenced the iconographic representation of Rome's founding heroes. Gagliardi (2023a) notes that in his duels after the death of Pallas, Aeneas often addresses his dying or dead adversaries with cruel words, in contrast to their appeals to his pietas.
